attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with painful leg ulcers that have been present for two weeks. He works as a labourer and recently sustained minor injuries to his legs at a construction site, but did not seek medical assistance. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He lives alone in temporary accommodation with limited access to washing facilities after a recent nasty separation.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple round ulcers on both lower legs with thick adherent grey-brown crusts and surrounding erythema. The ulcers are 1-2 cm in diameter with punched-out edges that extend into the dermis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with ecthyma, which is a deep bacterial infection of the skin that extends into the dermis. It is typically caused by Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Streptococcus) or Staphylococcus aureus. The characteristic appearance includes ulcers with punched-out edges and thick adherent crusts, typically over the lower limbs. Risk factors include poor hygiene, minor trauma, and poor living conditions, all of which are present in this case. Patients who are immunosuppressed are also at risk.

Initial treatment also includes removing the crusted area and managing the risk factors of ectyhma. The underlying social factors contributing to poor hygiene should also be addressed to prevent recurrence.

Oral flucloxacillin is the most appropriate treatment as systemic antibiotics are required due to the depth of infection. The duration of treatment is typically 7-14 days. Unlike superficial impetigo, topical antibiotics alone are insufficient due to the depth of invasion into the dermis.

Topical mupirocin alone would be inadequate for treating ecthyma as it cannot penetrate deeply enough to reach the infection in the dermis. While it may be used as an adjunct to systemic therapy, it should not be the primary treatment.

Topical hydrocortisone would be inappropriate as it may worsen the infection by suppressing the local immune response. Corticosteroids are contraindicated in untreated bacterial infections.

Compression bandaging alone would not address the underlying infection. While good wound care is important, treating the bacterial infection is the priority.

Topical fusidic acid, like other topical antibiotics, would be insufficient to treat this deep infection. Systemic antibiotics are required to achieve adequate tissue penetration.
learningPoints: Ecthyma is a deep bacterial skin infection that extends into the dermis, typically caused by Group A Streptococcus or Staphylococcus aureus.
Poor hygiene, minor trauma, and immunocompromise are risk factors for developing ecthyma.
references: Ngan V, Gomez J. Ecthyma. DermNet NZ. 2003.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/ecthyma
Ecthyma (including ecthyma gangrenosum). Primary Care Dermatology Society for GPS. 2021. Available at: https://www.pcds.org.uk/clinical-guidance/ecthyma-gangrenosum
